perturbation.original_text: 2.4 And undertakes not to disclose any of such information to any third party without the prior written permission of the other party except as required by any applicable law or government regulation, and provided that each party may disclose the agreement on a confidential basis to its public accountants, attorneys and financial advisors and/or funders.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.4 And undertakes not to disclose any of such information to any third party without the prior written permission of the other party except when deemed necessary, and provided that each party may disclose the agreement on a confidential basis to its public accountants, attorneys and financial advisors and/or funders.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'as required by any applicable law or government regulation' with 'when deemed necessary' introduces ambiguity. What is 'deemed necessary' is subjective and undefined, potentially leading to unauthorized disclosures. This creates an ambiguous obligation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Protection of Personal Information Act 4 of 2013 (POPIA)
perturbation.law_citation: Section 14(1) of the Protection of Personal Information Act
perturbation.law_explanation: POPIA sets strict conditions for the lawful processing of personal information, including limitations on disclosure. The modification allows disclosure 'when deemed necessary' without defining the necessity or adhering to the lawful processing conditions prescribed by POPIA, thus creating a potential contradiction.

perturbation.justification: The original text allowed disclosure when 'required by any applicable law or government regulation'. The changed text replaces this with 'when deemed necessary'. This introduces a subjective criterion for disclosure, potentially conflicting with legal obligations under POPIA, which mandates adherence to lawful processing conditions, according to the Law Explanation. The original clause ensured compliance with legal requirements, while the altered clause lacks such assurance, making the contract ambiguous and potentially non-compliant. This aligns with the definition of Ambiguity Legal Contradiction, which is that an ambiguous obligation makes it difficult to enforce under state or national law.

perturbation.original_text: 3.2 Any Business Partner shall maintain the material as confidential and shall not publish, disclose or distribute all or any portion of it (or any copies of it) to any other person or permit any other person access to it, save for employees, consultants or agents of the Business Partner for purposes specifically related to Mandarin’s use of the material.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.2 Any Business Partner shall endeavor to maintain the material as confidential and shall not publish, disclose or distribute all or any portion of it (or any copies of it) to any other person or permit any other person access to it, save for employees, consultants or agents of the Business Partner for purposes specifically related to Mandarin’s use of the material.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall maintain' to 'shall endeavor to maintain' weakens the obligation to maintain confidentiality. 'Endeavor' implies an attempt, not a guarantee, thus creating ambiguity regarding the level of commitment required to keep the material confidential. This creates an ambiguous obligation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Protection of Personal Information Act 4 of 2013 (POPIA)
perturbation.law_citation: Section 19 of the Protection of Personal Information Act
perturbation.law_explanation: POPIA requires responsible parties to secure the integrity and confidentiality of personal information in their possession or under their control by taking appropriate, reasonable technical and organizational measures. Changing 'shall maintain' to 'shall endeavor to maintain' weakens the obligation and could fail to meet the reasonable measures requirement under POPIA.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Protection of Personal Information Act, 2013 - Wikipedia Jump to content From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia Protection of Personal Information Act, 2013 Parliament of South Africa Long title Act to promote the protection of personal information processed by public and private bodies; to introduce certain conditions so as to establish minimum requirements for the processing of personal information; to provide for the establishment of an Information Regulator to exercise certain powers and to perform certain duties and functions in terms of this Act and the Promotion of Access to Information Act, 2000; to provide for the issuing of codes of conduct; to provide for the rights of persons regarding unsolicited electronic communications and automated decision making; to regulate the flow of personal information across the borders of the Republic; and to provide for matters connected therewith. Citation Act No. 4 of 2013 Territorial extent Republic of South Africa Enacted by Parliament of South Africa Assented to 19 November 2013 Commenced 1 July 2020 Keywords data protection right to privacy Status: In force South African privacy legislation This article is about South African privacy legislation. For the food, see Popiah . For the 1976 TV series, see Popi (TV series) . For the Greek singer, see Popi Malliotaki . The Protection of Personal Information Act ( PoPIA or the PoPI Act ) is a piece of legislation which governs the law of data protection and privacy in South Africa . [ 1 ] The act was passed to regulate the right to privacy, as enshrined by section 14 of the Constitution of South Africa , and would work in conjunction with the Promotion of Access to Information Act . The President of South Africa assented to the Act on 19 November 2013. As part of the regulation a new government agency was created, the Information Regulator, [ 2 ] an independent body which is empowered to monitor and enforce compliance of the PoPI Act within the public and private sector. The act came into force 1 July 2020, which commenced a one-year grace period during which all South African entities were expected to become compliant. The grace period ended 30 June 2021, with the commencement of the act on the 1 July 2021. [ 3 ] [ 4 ] [ 5 ] Core Obligations [ edit ] The PoPI Act sets out several core obligations. [ 6 ] Some of the key requirements include: Personal information can only be processed: with the consent of the data subject; or if it is necessary for the conclusion or performance of a contract that a data subject is a party to; or it is required by law; or it protects a legitimate interest of a data subject; or if processing is necessary for pursuing the legitimate interests of the responsible party or of a third party to whom the information is supplied. Private and public entities must report data leaks to the affected people and the Information Regulator. Organisations must appoint a responsible person who must ensure compliance to the PoPI Act. Cross-border transfers of personal da
perturbation.justification: The change from "shall maintain" to "shall endeavor to maintain" weakens the obligation to protect confidential information. The Law Explanation states that this could fail to meet the reasonable measures requirement under POPIA. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is absent, Scraped Law Snippet 2 references the Protection of Personal Information Act (PoPIA) and its purpose to establish minimum requirements for processing personal information. The modification introduces ambiguity, and the weakening of the obligation directly undermines the intent of POPIA to ensure the integrity and confidentiality of personal information by requiring responsible parties to take reasonable measures.

perturbation.original_text: 4.1 The Business Partner agrees that access to ResRequest information will be used for the sole use of the Business Partner and no auxiliary distribution is permitted, unless agreed upon by both parties.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.1 The Business Partner agrees that access to ResRequest information will ideally be used for the sole use of the Business Partner and auxiliary distribution may be considered if deemed appropriate by the Business Partner, unless explicitly prohibited by both parties.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying 'will be used for the sole use...and no auxiliary distribution is permitted' to 'will ideally be used for the sole use...and auxiliary distribution may be considered if deemed appropriate by the Business Partner' introduces significant ambiguity. The terms 'ideally' and 'deemed appropriate' are subjective and weaken the restriction on distribution. This creates an ambiguous obligation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Protection of Personal Information Act 4 of 2013 (POPIA)
perturbation.law_citation: Section 8 of the Protection of Personal Information Act
perturbation.law_explanation: POPIA requires that personal information be processed lawfully and in a reasonable manner that does not infringe the privacy of the data subject. This modification allows for potentially uncontrolled distribution of ResRequest information (potentially containing personal information) based on what the Business Partner 'deems appropriate,' conflicting with POPIA's requirement for lawful and reasonable processing.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: of the regulation a new government agency was created, the Information Regulator, [ 2 ] an independent body which is empowered to monitor and enforce compliance of the PoPI Act within the public and private sector. The act came into force 1 July 2020, which commenced a one-year grace period during which all South African entities were expected to become compliant. The grace period ended 30 June 2021, with the commencement of the act on the 1 July 2021. [ 3 ] [ 4 ] [ 5 ] Core Obligations [ edit ] The PoPI Act sets out several core obligations. [ 6 ] Some of the key requirements include: Personal information can only be processed: with the consent of the data subject; or if it is necessary for the conclusion or performance of a contract that a data subject is a party to; or it is required by law; or it protects a legitimate interest of a data subject; or if processing is necessary for pursuing the legitimate interests of the responsible party or of a third party to whom the information is supplied. Private and public entities must report data leaks to the affected people and the Information Regulator. Organisations must appoint a responsible person who must ensure compliance to the PoPI Act. Cross-border transfers of personal data are restricted. Organisations that process personal information must ensure they satisfy minimum security obligations. Direct marketing, the sale and use of electronic directories and automated decision making are also severely curtailed. The act elevates the obligations placed on entities that process information regarding children, religious beliefs, race, ethnic origin, trade union membership, health, sex life, criminal behaviour and biometric information. Jurisdiction [ edit ] The PoPI Act applies to all persons and organisations within the borders of South Africa, and extends to visitors and illegal immigrants. [ 7 ] Penalties [ edit ] Penalties under the Act include fines of up to R 10 million and a jail sentence of up to 10 years. [ 8 ] In July 2023, The Information Regulator fined the Department of Justice and Constitutional Development R5 million rand. [ 9 ] [ 10 ] Information Regulator Agency overview Formed 1 July 2020 ( 2020-07-01 ) Jurisdiction South Africa Headquarters JD House, 27 Stiemens Street, Braamfontein , Johannesburg , 2001 Agency executives Adv. Pansy Tlakula, Chairperson Mosalanyane Mosala, CEO Website inforegulator .org .za Information Regulator [ edit ] The Information Regulator is an independent body created in response to the PoPI and PAIA acts. It is empowered to monitor and enforce compliance of the acts within the public and private sector. It functions in terms of the two acts and is accountable to the National Assembly of South Africa . Cybercrimes Act [ edit ] South Africa does not yet have a formal cohesive piece of legislation in force which governs cybercrimes in South Africa. The Cybercrimes Act has been signed by the President of South Africa , and will come into force on a date to be proclaimed in the Government Gazette . The period between assent and commencement will be spent developing operating procedures and other documented processes for the implementation of provisions of the Act. "SA's ne
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces ambiguity regarding the distribution of ResRequest information, which may include personal data. The original text strictly prohibits auxiliary distribution unless agreed upon by both parties. The changed text weakens this restriction by stating that auxiliary distribution 'may be considered if deemed appropriate by the Business Partner,' introducing subjective criteria. The Law Explanation states that this conflicts with POPIA's requirement for lawful and reasonable processing of personal information. Scraped Law Snippet 2 supports this by outlining core obligations of the PoPI Act, including processing personal information lawfully and reasonably. The modification creates a potential for uncontrolled distribution based on the Business Partner's subjective assessment, contradicting the principle of lawful and reasonable processing under POPIA. This ambiguity introduces a legal contradiction by undermining the original stricture.

perturbation.original_text: 5.1 Neither party shall be liable to the other for any special, indirect or consequential damages or loss.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.1 Neither party is expected to be liable to the other for any special, indirect or consequential damages or loss.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall be liable' to 'is expected to be liable' significantly weakens the disclaimer of liability. 'Expected' implies a possibility rather than a definite exclusion of liability, thus creating ambiguity as to the extent of liability coverage. This creates an ambiguous obligation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Contract Law Principles under South African Law.
perturbation.law_citation: General Principles of Contract Law in South Africa
perturbation.law_explanation: South African contract law requires clear and unambiguous terms, especially regarding liability. While disclaimers of liability are generally permissible, they must be clearly worded to exclude liability fully. The modified text, using 'is expected,' creates uncertainty about whether liability is actually excluded. This ambiguity could lead a court to interpret the clause against the party seeking to rely on it, effectively undermining the intended exclusion of liability.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Life and ‘The Scriptures’ in John 5:39-40 Skip to main content Life and ‘The Scriptures’ in John 5:39-40 Loading... Files dissertation.pdf (203.55 KB) Date 2007-09-26 Authors Journal Title Journal ISSN Volume Title Publisher University of Pretoria Abstract In John 5:39-40 we see a dividing line being drawn over the proper interpretation of “the Scriptures” between the Jewish leaders of the first century and the early Christians of the Johannine community. Both parties agreed that “searching the Scriptures” was essential; however, the two groups disagreed about the nature with which one was to “search the Scriptures” and how one was to possess or lay hold of this eternal life. “The Jews” viewed eternal life as a birthright that was maintained by rigorous study of the law and strict observance of its principles. The study of the law became an end in itself. The Christians of the Johannine community, on the other hand, believed that eternal life was only granted as gift when a person comes to the one of whom the Scriptures testified, Jesus Christ. Eternal life was neither a birthright nor something that could be merited, but rather a gift given to those who truly “search the Scriptures” and truly see the one who they are written of. This disagreement between the Johannine community and the first century Jewish leadership is a reflection of the division between Jesus and the Jewish leaders during his day as well as a reflection of the division between Christians and Jews in our day. But perhaps more importantly, it is a reflection of the complacent attitude that pervades much of the church today. The words of this passage are a warning or wake-up call to us all. We must be careful not to presume that eternal life is ours because of any merit or association. We must be careful to search “the Scriptures” with our eyes wide open and without presuppositions that will hinder of from discerning its true meaning. But most importantly, we must look to the one of whom these “Scriptures” testify, Jesus Christ. If we truly want to live, as we assume all men do, then we must do what is necessary to inherit eternal life. We must heed the words of “the Scriptures” and we must heed Jesus’ words: we must come to Jesus who alone is the source of eternal life. This begins and ends with proper interpretation of the Word of God. Description Dissertation (MA (New Testament Studies))--University of Pretoria, 2007. Keywords Law , Witness , Testify , Come , Search , Jews , Eternal life , Scriptures , Interpret , Have/hold/possess , UCTD Sustainable Development Goals Citation Punch, JD 2007, Life and ‘The Scriptures’ in John 5:39-40, MA Dissertation, University of Pretoria, Pretoria, viewed yymmdd <http://hdl.handle.net/2263/28008> URI http://hdl.handle.net/2263/28008 Collections Theses and Dissertations (University of Pretoria) Theses and Dissertations (New Testament Studies) Full item page
perturbation.justification: The change from 'shall be liable' to 'is expected to be liable' introduces ambiguity, weakening the original disclaimer. The Law Explanation states South African contract law requires clear and unambiguous terms, particularly regarding liability disclaimers. The altered text creates uncertainty, potentially leading a court to interpret the clause against the party seeking to rely on it. This directly contradicts the requirement for clear exclusion of liability. Scraped law snippets are irrelevant.
